title: History, Objects, References, and Forensic Inspection · Repository Checkpoint
summary: Explain when and why a behavior changed using graph, path, object, author, and content evidence. Every item uses a fresh case and misconception-specific distractors. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a history-forensics evidence packet. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Query commit graph topology, decorations, authorship, dates, messages, and changed paths with an intentional log format.
Relate blobs, trees, commits, tags, object IDs, content identity, and repository reachability.
Inspect commits, trees, blobs, tags, and object types without checking out or executing repository content.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-basics cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Diagnose the hidden failure
steps.instruction:
quiz case — History has commits a11ce00 → b00b1e5 → c0ffee6; a file moved from notes.txt to docs/notes.txt with unchanged bytes. Explain graph, object identity, path evidence, and blame limits.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for use blame as a line-origin clue and connect it to surrounding commits, review context, and later movement without assigning fault.

content.text: History has commits a11ce00 → b00b1e5 → c0ffee6; a file moved from notes.txt to docs/notes.txt with unchanged bytes. Explain graph, object identity, path evidence, and blame limits.

content.title: Fault-isolation decision
content.text: Select evidence that identifies a cause, not a patch that only hides the Blame Context symptom.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: githooks Manual — Controls client and server hook invocation, environment, parameters, bypass, and exit behavior.

hints: Name the Blame Context precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: History has commits a11ce00 → b00b1e5 → c0ffee6; a file moved from notes.txt to docs/notes.txt with unchanged bytes. Explain graph, object identity, path evidence, and blame limits.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: The author shown by blame necessarily introduced the current bug.

stimulus.title: Blame Context failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: The author shown by blame necessarily introduced the current bug.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Use blame as a line-origin clue and connect it to surrounding commits, review context, and later movement without assigning fault.

options.text: Run the boundary case, inspect the actual mechanism, then accept or repair the claim.
Accept the result because visible output is the highest authority.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.

steps.title: Reconstruct the defensible process
steps.instruction: Order the Log Graph Format evidence cycle for this quiz task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Log Graph Format assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: History has commits a11ce00 → b00b1e5 → c0ffee2; a file moved from notes.txt to docs/notes.txt with unchanged bytes. Explain graph, object identity, path evidence, and blame limits.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Log Graph Format has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: Name the Log Graph Format precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: History has commits a11ce00 → b00b1e5 → c0ffee2; a file moved from notes.txt to docs/notes.txt with unchanged bytes. Explain graph, object identity, path evidence, and blame limits.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: git log lists commits in a universal causal order.

options.text: Inspect repository boundary, config origin, current operation, worktree, index, refs, and graph
Preserve uncommitted work and a recovery ref, then state intended tree and topology
Preview and perform the least destructive simulated Git operation
Verify status, staged and range diffs, graph, tests, remote target, and recovery path

steps.title: Defend one difficult decision
steps.instruction: Write the quiz evidence note for Blame Context: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — History has commits a11ce00 → b00b1e5 → c0ffee3; a file moved from notes.txt to docs/notes.txt with unchanged bytes. Explain graph, object identity, path evidence, and blame limits.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Use blame as a line-origin clue and connect it to surrounding commits, review context, and later movement without assigning fault.

hints: Name the Blame Context precondition or invariant that the quiz case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: History has commits a11ce00 → b00b1e5 → c0ffee3; a file moved from notes.txt to docs/notes.txt with unchanged bytes. Explain graph, object identity, path evidence, and blame limits.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: The author shown by blame necessarily introduced the current bug.
